main_ingredients: grilled unagi (freshwater eel) with kabayaki sauce
simmered beef slices in sweet soy-based sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately 650 g, comprising a double portion of grilled unagi over steamed rice with simmered beef, a half boiled egg, and a bowl of miso soup.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: Eel, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175136); Beef, short loin, top loin steak, separable lean only, trimmed to 0 inch fat, cooked, broiled (FDC ID 174035); White rice, cooked (FDC ID 169756); Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled (FDC ID 173424); Miso soup, prepared (FDC ID 172459); Kabayaki sauce estimated from standard recipe proportions.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrients estimated by weighted component assembly: approximately 200 g cooked unagi (kabayaki), 150 g simmered beef, 250 g steamed white rice, 30 g half boiled egg, 150 g miso soup, 20 g kabayaki sauce. Values expressed per 100 g of the assembled dish at estimated total weight of 650 g. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods used for all components.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white rice and mixed rice-based meals; analogous estimate based on steamed white rice (GI 64–72) as dominant carbohydrate component, moderated by protein and fat from eel and beef.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for unagi-gyudon donburi. GI estimated at 65 based on steamed Japanese short-grain white rice (GI 64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Am J Clin Nutr) as the primary glycemic carbohydrate source (~250 g of 650 g total serving). Protein and fat from eel and beef attenuate postprandial glucose response, supporting a mid-range estimate of 65. Glycemic load calculated as: GI 65 × carbohydrate per serving (133.25 g from 650 g × 20.5 g/100 g) / 100 = 86.6, rounded to 86.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from kabayaki sauce, soy-based beef broth, and miso soup
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to an estimated sodium content of approximately 310 mg per 100 g (approximately 2,015 mg per full serving), which substantially exceeds the recommended single-meal sodium target of 600–800 mg; requesting reduced sauce and omitting miso soup can lower sodium by an estimated 30–40%.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load per serving driven by a large portion of white rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this dish as the estimated glycemic load of approximately 86.5 per serving is very high; substituting brown rice or reducing the rice portion by half, combined with the protein and fat from eel and beef which slow gastric emptying, can meaningfully reduce postprandial blood glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium, partially offset by cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids from unagi
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be aware that while unagi provides beneficial long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) that support cardiovascular health, the high sodium content from soy-based sauces and miso soup poses a significant risk for blood pressure elevation, and portion control with reduced-sauce options is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Double Unagi Bowl with Beef Combo (ダブルうな牛丼コンボ) is a Japanese donburi rice bowl dish served at Sukiya-style Japanese fast-casual restaurants in Singapore. It features a double portion of grilled freshwater eel (unagi) prepared in the kabayaki style — butterflied, skewered, grilled over charcoal or gas, steamed, and re-grilled with a sweet soy-mirin-sake glaze — layered over a generous bed of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice alongside simmered sliced beef cooked in a sweet soy-based sauce (gyudon-style). The dish is served with a half boiled egg and a bowl of miso soup, making it a substantial, protein-rich, high-calorie meal. The flavour profile is umami-forward, sweet, and savoury, with the eel contributing a rich, slightly smoky, buttery texture and the beef adding tender, juicy depth. Nutritionally, this dish is energy-dense at approximately 168 kcal per 100 g, providing meaningful protein (~9.8 g/100 g) and notable fat (~5.2 g/100 g), with carbohydrates (~20.5 g/100 g) predominantly from white rice. Unagi is a notable dietary source of vitamin D, vitamin B12, vitamin A, and omega-3 fatty acids. The dish carries a high glycemic load per serving (~86.5) due to the large rice portion, and is high in sodium (~310 mg/100 g; ~2,015 mg per serving) from kabayaki sauce, beef broth, and miso soup, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with fish, soy, wheat, egg, or gluten allergies.

kabayaki: grilled eel with sweet soy glaze

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific composite dish (double unagi-gyudon donburi). Nutrient values were estimated by weighted component assembly using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods data for each major ingredient (cooked eel, simmered beef, steamed white rice, boiled egg, miso soup, kabayaki sauce). GI was estimated analogously from University of Sydney GI Database data for white rice as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is systematic and source-grounded, but the exact restaurant recipe proportions, sauce quantities, and cooking methods at Sukiya Singapore are not publicly documented in an approved source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-assembly estimates per 100 g of the full assembled dish at an estimated total serving weight of 650 g (200 g unagi kabayaki, 150 g simmered beef, 250 g steamed white rice, 30 g half boiled egg, 150 g miso soup, 20 g kabayaki sauce). 2. Sodium estimate is conservative; actual sodium may be higher depending on sauce volumes used by the restaurant. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in approved sources. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not directly measured for this dish. 5. This dish is not halal-certified as it contains mirin (alcohol) and the halal status of the eel and beef sourcing at Sukiya Singapore is not confirmed in an approved source. 6. The dish is listed as item 983 on the field survey data menu for Sukiya - Funan and Funan - Mix & Match. 7. Price at time of data collection: SGD 27.30. 8. No -1 sentinel values are used for core macros; all secondary nutrient -1 values have been replaced with estimated numeric values from USDA component data, so no -1 sentinel documentation is required.
